productLifecycle.label: Acquire evidence
productLifecycle.description: Load Java/Kotlin bytecode or TypeScript analysis artifacts and let AtlasArc build the dependency model. No annotations or application-source changes.

productLifecycle.label: Define scope
productLifecycle.description: Start with the project as it is. Commit a shared scope when the IDE, reports, and CI need to evaluate the same evidence.

productLifecycle.label: Explore
productLifecycle.description: Move between topology, matrix, composition, hotspots, and metrics without leaving IntelliJ. Zoom out across the system or pin the package you care about.

productLifecycle.label: Identify cycles
productLifecycle.kicker: Untangle one cycle at a time
productLifecycle.description: Isolate related loops in the cycle problem graph, then follow the exact dependency edge back to the source that created it.

productLifecycle.label: Govern decisions
productLifecycle.description: Mark a dependency as intentional or tracked debt. The reason and ticket live with the code, where the team can review them.

productLifecycle.label: Enforce in CI
productLifecycle.description: Run the same evaluator through the standalone CLI or ArchUnit/JUnit. Known cycles pass; new ones fail with evidence you can reproduce locally.

productLifecycle.label: Report
productLifecycle.description: Turn the evidence and decisions from your investigation into a browsable Architecture Audit Report, with a printable version for reviews and planning.

productLifecycle.label: Share investigations
productLifecycle.description: Save the lens, scope, filters, and subject. A teammate can reopen the same question without reconstructing your setup.

workflows.title: Find cyclic package dependencies
workflows.description: Detect the dependency loops that block refactoring before they compound. Navigate from the graph directly to the class breaking the cycle.

workflows.title: Inspect cycle edges in the matrix
workflows.description: Use the Package Matrix to read the directional edges that keep a cycle group alive after the graph has found the loop.

workflows.title: Review package boundaries
workflows.description: See which packages cross intended architectural boundaries and how much traffic flows across them. Catch boundary erosion before it spreads.

workflows.title: Identify risky hotspots
workflows.description: Plot complexity, coupling, volatility, and architectural distance to find the packages where refactoring pays off most.

workflows.title: Understand subsystem coupling
workflows.description: Plot subsystems in metric space to see which parts of the architecture are stable, which are under pressure, and where coupling is highest.

workflows.title: Show me the backbone
workflows.description: Find the packages the whole system would collapse without: high fan-in, stable, and at the centre of every dependency chain.

workflows.title: Find suspicious dependents
workflows.description: Spot packages that are importing things they have no business touching: wrong layer, wrong module, wrong abstraction level.

workflows.title: Stable but too concrete
workflows.description: Find packages that are stable enough that nothing will change them, but concrete enough that they are a maintenance trap.

workflows.title: Find accidental boundary leaks
workflows.description: Detect where implementation packages are reachable from outside their intended module boundary.

workflows.title: Packages in the wrong place
workflows.description: Identify packages that don't belong where they are: the wrong layer, the wrong module, or the wrong abstraction level for their actual dependency direction.

workflows.title: Cycle / tangle reduction
workflows.description: Work through a backlog of dependency cycles methodically over multiple sprints, tracking progress as the problem graph shrinks.

workflows.title: Zone of Pain
workflows.description: Investigate packages in the Zone of Pain: maximally stable, maximally concrete, and hard to change without breaking everything that depends on them.

workflows.title: Zone of Uselessness
workflows.description: Find packages that are maximally abstract but have no dependents: over-engineered abstractions that the rest of the system is not using.

workflows.title: Leaky internals
workflows.description: Find packages exposing far more internal surface than callers need, then inspect the local class/file evidence inside the graph.

workflows.title: Find hermit packages
workflows.description: Spot isolated packages with no dependencies in either direction: dead code, a forgotten refactor, or a self-contained utility ready to promote.

workflows.title: Hard-to-change packages
workflows.description: Find the packages where even small changes cause outsized ripple effects through the codebase.

workflows.title: Ticking time bombs
workflows.description: Catch the unstable packages quietly accumulating callers: cheap to refactor now, expensive once they harden into load-bearing cores.

workflows.title: Complexity hotspots
workflows.description: Find where reading difficulty concentrates at the class and method level instead of stopping at the package aggregate.

workflows.title: Complex code with weak coverage
workflows.description: Combine cyclomatic complexity with branch coverage to find risky packages that need characterization tests before refactoring.

workflows.title: Audit a module boundary
workflows.description: Do a thorough structural review of one module: what flows in, what flows out, package cycles inside, and how cohesive the implementation is.

workflows.title: Audit individual cycle groups
workflows.description: Step through every cycle group in the project one at a time and decide what to do with each.

workflows.title: Audit a subsystem
workflows.description: Get a complete structural health snapshot of one subsystem: size, complexity, visibility, stability, and coverage gaps in one session.

workflows.title: Manual coverage heatmap review
workflows.description: Load a JaCoCo coverage report and find which load-bearing packages the test suite is not exercising.

workflows.title: Project architecture health review
workflows.description: Get a project-level structural summary suitable for a team retrospective or technical debt planning session.

metrics.title: Cyclic dependencies
metrics.description: Count and classify dependency cycles as architectural, aggregation, or nested-internal. See which packages are entangled and which relationships form the cycle core.

metrics.title: Fan-in and fan-out
metrics.description: Measure how widely a package is used and how many dependencies it takes on. The primary signal for evaluating structural stability and coupling.

metrics.title: Instability and abstractness
metrics.description: Plot packages on the distance-from-main-sequence diagram to find which ones are in the zone of pain: stable, concrete, and hardest to change.

metrics.title: Relative visibility
metrics.description: Measure how much of a package's surface area is publicly exposed. High visibility in an implementation package is unwanted coupling waiting to happen.

metrics.title: Cyclomatic complexity
metrics.description: Count independent control-flow paths through methods. CC max reveals the worst offender; CC avg shows how widespread the complexity is across the package.

metrics.title: Cognitive complexity
metrics.description: Measure how hard code is to read by penalizing nesting and flow-breaking constructs. This is a closer proxy for actual reading difficulty than cyclomatic complexity.

metrics.title: Class count
metrics.description: Count the declared types in a package. The primary code-mass metric for understanding structural size before refactoring or extraction work.

metrics.title: Lines of code
metrics.description: Count source lines in a package or class. The fastest orientation metric for finding where the weight is in an unfamiliar codebase.

metrics.title: Method count
metrics.description: Count methods on a class. High method count is the fastest smell test for a class that has accumulated too many responsibilities over time.

metrics.title: Line and branch coverage
metrics.description: Surface JaCoCo line and branch coverage as architecture heatmaps. Find which load-bearing packages the test suite is not actually exercising.

lenses.title: Topology Graph
lenses.description: Every package as a node, every usage dependency as a directed edge, with package-local class/file internals when one package needs closer inspection.

lenses.title: Package Matrix
lenses.description: A package/source-folder matrix that makes directionality and mutual coupling obvious where the graph hides it.

lenses.title: Composition
lenses.description: A treemap, sunburst, or circle pack by class count for seeing where the code actually lives.

lenses.title: Subsystems
lenses.description: Architectural areas compared in metric space so you can reason about boundaries at a coarser granularity.

lenses.title: Hotspots
lenses.description: A metric-space lens with bubble and Galaxy views for finding packages or classes where complexity, coupling, size, coverage, and stability risk overlap.

languages.title: Java
languages.description: Which Java language constructs AtlasArc recognizes as package dependencies, and which source-looking relationships are deliberately out of scope.

languages.title: Kotlin
languages.description: How AtlasArc reads Kotlin dependencies through compiled JVM output, including top-level declarations, delegation, value classes, and suspend functions.

languages.title: TypeScript
languages.description: How to analyze TypeScript projects with a generated dependency graph, optional ESLint/SonarJS SARIF, and manually loaded LCOV coverage.

workflowBeats.label: Map
workflowBeats.title: Explore the structure
workflowBeats.intro: Before diagnosis, read the architecture model: packages as a graph, dependencies as a matrix, and code mass as composition. Set the right root, expand the right packages, and get oriented before deciding what deserves attention.

workflowBeats.label: Diagnose
workflowBeats.title: Diagnose structural problems
workflowBeats.intro: Find cycles, boundary leaks, misplaced packages, and internals that have become part of the public architecture by accident.

workflowBeats.label: Expose
workflowBeats.title: Expose complex, undertested code
workflowBeats.intro: Combine complexity, coverage, and package structure to find the code that needs characterization tests before refactoring starts.

workflowBeats.label: Prioritize
workflowBeats.title: Prioritize refactoring work
workflowBeats.intro: Decide where effort pays off by looking at coupling pressure, stability, abstraction, complexity, size, and the parts of the architecture other packages depend on.

workflowBeats.label: Govern
workflowBeats.title: Govern intentional exceptions
workflowBeats.intro: After triage, record intentional dependencies and managed debt in AtlasArc's committed governance file, then enforce the same decisions in CI. Safe Havens remain local investigation tools; repository governance is reviewable team policy.

workflowBeats.label: Report
workflowBeats.title: Review and report
workflowBeats.intro: Turn an investigation into an Architecture Audit Report the team can use: project health baselines, subsystem audits, boundary reviews, and focused evidence for the decision at hand.
